
---
title: "Network Intrusion Detection Using CIC-IDS-2017"
author: "Group E: Vishal Kumar, Avanti Gupta, Shivangi Manel"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

## 1. Introduction

This project investigates network intrusion detection using the CIC-IDS-2017 
dataset published by the Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity. Cyberattacks 
pose a growing threat to modern infrastructure, and the ability to automatically 
classify malicious network traffic is a critical challenge in cybersecurity. 
The goal of this analysis is to determine whether network flow features can be 
used to accurately identify and classify different types of cyberattacks using 
machine learning models in R.

## 2. Dataset Description

The CIC-IDS-2017 dataset consists of eight CSV files capturing network traffic 
recorded over five days (Monday through Friday) in a controlled lab environment. 
After combining all files and removing rows with infinite or missing values, the 
final cleaned dataset contains 2,827,876 observations and 79 variables. The 78 
feature variables are all quantitative and describe network flow statistics such 
as packet lengths, flow duration, inter-arrival times, byte rates, and TCP flag 
counts. The target variable, Label, is categorical and identifies each flow as 
either benign or one of 13 attack types including DDoS, PortScan, DoS Hulk, 
FTP-Patator, SSH-Patator, Bot, and Web Attacks. An additional implicit 
categorical variable is the time period (day of week) from which each file 
originates.

## 3. Research Questions

This analysis investigates the following questions. First, can network flow 
features accurately classify traffic as benign or as a specific attack type? 
Second, which features are the most important predictors of attack type? 
Third, how does class imbalance affect model performance, and which model 
handles minority attack classes better — Random Forest or XGBoost?

## 4. Descriptive Statistics

The table below summarizes key quantitative features across the dataset. 
Flow Bytes per second shows an extremely large range (from negative values 
caused by zero-duration flows up to 2 billion), reflecting the diverse nature 
of network traffic. Packet Length Standard Deviation and Average Packet Size 
similarly show high variance, suggesting that attack traffic differs 
substantially from normal traffic in its packet structure. Flow IAT Mean 
(inter-arrival time) ranges from near zero to 120 million microseconds, 
capturing both high-speed attack floods and slow-rate attacks.

![Descriptive Statistics](plot5_desc_stats.png)

## 5. Class Distribution

The dataset is severely imbalanced, as shown in the figure below. Benign 
traffic accounts for over 2.2 million records, while some attack types are 
extremely rare — Infiltration has only 36 samples and SQL Injection has only 
21 samples across the entire dataset. This imbalance is a fundamental 
challenge for classification, as models trained on such data tend to favor 
the majority class. A log scale is used on the y-axis to make minority 
classes visible.

![Class Distribution](plot1_class_distribution.png)

## 6. Data Preprocessing

Several preprocessing steps were applied before modeling. Columns with zero 
variance were removed as they carry no predictive information. Features with 
pairwise correlation above 0.95 were also removed to reduce redundancy, 
as shown in the correlation heatmap. Min-max normalization was applied to 
all remaining numeric features to place them on a common scale. A stratified 
10% sample of 282,781 rows was drawn to make model training computationally 
feasible, and classes with fewer than three samples (Heartbleed and SQL 
Injection) were excluded from modeling. The data was then split into 80% 
training (226,230 rows) and 20% testing (56,550 rows) using stratified 
partitioning.

## 7. Feature Importance

After training the Random Forest model, feature importance was assessed using 
the Mean Decrease in Gini impurity. The figure below shows the top 20 most 
important predictors. Packet Length Standard Deviation, Average Backward 
Segment Size, and Packet Length Variance are the three most discriminative 
features. This suggests that attacks are most distinguishable from benign 
traffic by the variability and size of their packets rather than their timing 
or port usage. Destination Port, while ranked 7th, also plays a meaningful 
role, consistent with the fact that certain attacks target specific ports.

![Feature Importance](plot2_feature_importance.png)

## 8. Model Results

Two classification models were trained and compared: Random Forest with 500 
trees and XGBoost with 100 boosting rounds. The Random Forest model achieved 
an overall out-of-bag error rate of 0.2%, indicating excellent overall 
performance. On the test set, both models achieved near-perfect F1 scores for 
well-represented classes such as BENIGN (0.999), DDoS (0.999), FTP-Patator 
(1.000), and PortScan (0.996). However, performance differed notably on 
minority classes. XGBoost achieved an F1 score of 0.75 for Bot traffic 
compared to 0.52 for Random Forest, demonstrating that gradient boosting 
handles class imbalance better in this context. Classes with very few samples 
such as Infiltration and Web Attack XSS could not be reliably detected by 
either model, yielding NA or near-zero F1 scores.

![F1 Score Comparison](plot3_f1_comparison.png)

## 9. ROC Curve Analysis

The ROC curve below evaluates the Random Forest model ability to distinguish 
benign traffic from any attack type. The model achieved an AUC of 0.9998, 
indicating near-perfect discrimination. The curve rises sharply to the 
top-left corner, confirming that the model can detect attacks with very high 
sensitivity while maintaining an extremely low false positive rate. This is 
particularly important in real network environments where falsely flagging 
benign traffic would disrupt normal operations.

![ROC Curve](plot4_roc.png)

## 10. Conclusion

This analysis demonstrates that machine learning models trained on network 
flow statistics can classify cyberattacks with high accuracy. Both Random 
Forest and XGBoost achieved near-perfect performance on well-represented 
attack types, with overall error rates below 0.2%. XGBoost outperformed 
Random Forest on minority attack classes, making it the preferred model 
for real-world deployment where rare attacks are most dangerous. The primary 
limitation of this analysis is severe class imbalance — attack types with 
very few samples could not be reliably detected. Future work should explore 
oversampling techniques such as SMOTE to improve minority class detection. 
The most important predictors were packet length statistics, confirming that 
attacks generate distinctive packet size patterns that distinguish them from 
normal traffic.
